generations.text:
**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with trembling hands and a heart full of sorrow, for the wind howls like a banshee through the stones of this old lighthouse, and the sea roars with a fury that makes me fear I may not see another dawn. Yet, in this moment of dread, I find myself compelled to reach out—to you, my child, my only daughter, whom I have not seen nor spoken to in five long, cruel years.

I know you do not wish to hear from me. I know you have closed your heart to me, just as I have closed mine to the world. But tonight, as the tempest gathers strength and the waves rise like vengeful spirits against the cliffs, I feel the weight of years pressing upon me, and I am reminded that I have not yet said what I must.

Eleanor, I do not write this to beg your forgiveness, for I know that forgiveness is not something I deserve. I have been a failure as a father, a husband, a man. I let duty consume me, I let pride blind me, and I let silence grow between us like a wall of stone. I have not been there for you, and for that, I am truly sorry.

I was so proud when you left, when you chose your own path, when you left this desolate coast behind for the life of a city, for the life of a woman. I did not understand then, and I

**The Lighthouse, Stormhaven**  
**October 12th, 1894**  

My Dearest Eliza,  

I write this with a quill that trembles in my hand, and the wind howls like a wounded beast outside. The sea roars in fury, and the waves crash against the cliff with a violence I have never known. I fear for my life, and I fear for yours more.  

Five long years have passed since you left this house, and I have not heard a word from you. Not a single letter, not a whisper of your voice. I have tried to forget, to pretend that you were never my daughter, but the memories cling to me like the salt upon my skin. I see your face in the flickering light of the beacon, I hear your laughter in the wind, and I feel your absence in every empty chair at the table.  

I do not know why you left, Eliza. I do not know if it was your heart or my own that turned cold. I tried to be the father you needed, but I fear I failed you. I tried to give you a life of comfort and security, but perhaps you longed for something more. Perhaps you thought I was too rigid, too stern, too much the keeper of the light and not the keeper of your soul.  

I have not spoken of you to the others, not even to the old man who tends the lantern. I

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with trembling hands, the wind howling like a beast in the night, and the sea, that ancient and unyielding thing, roiling in fury outside these walls. I have not spoken to you in five years, and yet, as the storm gathers strength, I find myself compelled to write—not to beg for forgiveness, not to plead for reconciliation, but to say the words I have long kept buried beneath the weight of silence.

You were ten when you left, Eleanor. A child still, with a heart too large for the world and a mind too sharp for the years ahead. You called me a tyrant, a man who saw only duty and not the boy you once were. You said I had forgotten how to love. And I suppose, in a way, I had. Or perhaps I had never known how to love you, not as a father, not as a man.

I have spent these years watching the sea, not with fear, but with reverence. The lighthouse is my life, my purpose, my reason for rising with the dawn. But tonight—tonight is different. The storm is unlike any I have seen in my time here. The waves rise like mountains, the wind screams with a voice that echoes in my bones. I do not know if I will see morning.

And yet, as I sit here, with the lantern burning and the lamp oil flickering in the dark, I think of

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with trembling hands and a heart heavy with years of silence. The wind howls outside like the very devil himself, and the sea roars with a fury that makes me fear I may not live to see the dawn. Yet, in this moment of impending chaos, I find myself thinking of you—my Eleanor, my only daughter, my greatest regret.

Five years have passed since you left, and five years have not been long enough to forget. You were once the light in my life, the soft glow of a candle in the darkest of nights. Now, that light has gone, and I have been left with only the cold glow of the lighthouse and the echo of your footsteps in the halls I no longer walk.

I know you do not want to hear from me, and I do not blame you. I have not been the father you deserved. I have been a man lost in his own sorrow, too proud to ask for forgiveness, too stubborn to reach out. I let the silence grow between us like the sea between the cliffs and the shore. And now, as the storm gathers, I am left with nothing but regret.

You were not the one who left, Eleanor. I was the one who turned away. I let my pride and my shame blind me to the truth of what I had done. I let the guilt of your mother’s passing consume me, and I forgot that you were still here, still waiting,

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with trembling hands and a heart heavier than the storm that now rages upon the cliffs. The sea howls like a wounded beast, and the wind screams as though it too knows the sorrow that has long since settled in my bones. I fear I may not see the dawn, and if I do not, I beg you—my darling girl—know that I have never stopped loving you, nor have I ever ceased to pray for your happiness.

Five years have passed since the last time we spoke, and I have counted each day like the flickering light of the lighthouse. You were a child then, and I, a man too proud to admit the ache of a father's heart. I told myself that distance was the only way to keep you safe, that the world was too cruel, too wild, and that I was not the man you needed. But I was wrong, Eleanor. I was not the man you needed. I was a man who let fear guide his hand and silence his voice.

You were not the only one who suffered. I have spent these years watching the sea, not just as a keeper, but as a man who has lost the ability to look upon the horizon with hope. The light I keep burning is not just for the ships—it is for you, my child. It is for the day I might see you again, if the stars and the sea will allow it.

The storm is coming,

**To My Dearest Eliza,**

I hope this letter finds you in the arms of some comfort, though I fear it may not. I write with a trembling hand, the wind howling like a banshee outside this lighthouse, and the sea, that old adversary, roars with a fury I've never known. The storm is upon us, and I can only pray that the light holds, that the lamp burns bright enough to guide the lost and the damned.

Five years, Eliza. Five long, cruel years since you left this house, this life, and this father behind you. I have not spoken your name in that time, not even in my prayers. I have not spoken to you at all. And yet, tonight, as the sky darkens and the waves crash like the thunder of war, I find myself thinking of you more than I have in years.

You were always a strange child, my little lark. You had a mind that danced where others walked, and a heart that beat to a tune I could not follow. I tried to understand, to guide you, but you were always turning your back on the path I had laid for you. You wanted the world to be something more than what it is, and I feared what that would mean for you. I feared for you.

I have always been a man of duty, Eliza. The lighthouse, the sea, the light—it is all I have ever known. You were

**To My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with trembling hands, the wind howling like a wounded beast against the stone walls of this old lighthouse. The sea is restless, and the sky is dark as midnight, heavy with storm and sorrow. I do not know if I shall live to see the dawn, but if I do, I hope this letter finds you.

Five years have passed since you left, Eleanor. Five long, lonely years. I have not spoken to you, not even in the silence of the night when the waves whisper secrets only the sea can keep. I have kept the light burning, as I always have, but my heart has long since gone out to sea with you.

I have tried, my child, to be the father you once needed. I have tried to be the man you once believed in. But time, like the tides, has worn me down. The loneliness of this place has taken its toll. I have no one to speak with, no one to share the burden of this duty. Even the old cat, Marmalade, has grown quiet in the face of this storm.

I have heard the stories, Eleanor. I have heard the whispers of the town, the murmurs that you no longer speak of me, that you no longer think of me. I have heard the rumors that you have moved on, that you have found a new life, a new home, a new father. I have heard it all

**The Lighthouse Keeper’s Letter**

**To My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with a trembling hand, the wind howling like a wounded beast outside these ancient walls. The sea roars with a fury I have never known, and I fear the night may claim me before dawn. Yet, even as I stand on the precipice of darkness, I cannot silence the voice in my heart that calls for you.

Five years have passed since you left this house, and five years have not dimmed the ache of your absence. I have watched the sun rise and set, the moon wax and wane, and still I wait for the day you return. I have kept the light burning for you, even when no ship was near, even when the world seemed to have forgotten the path you once walked.

I know you do not wish to see me, and I do not blame you. You were a child when you left, and I a man who failed to understand the weight of your sorrow. I was too wrapped in the solitude of the sea, too lost in the duties of this lonely post, to see the storm brewing in your heart. I was blind, Eleanor. I was blind, and I paid the price.

But tonight, as the heavens weep and the waves rise like a wrathful army, I am reminded that even the darkest night must end. And I fear that I may not live to see the dawn. I have been told the storm is unlike

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with trembling hands and a heart heavy with the weight of years. The sea howls like a vengeful spirit, and the wind screams through the cracks in this old lighthouse as though it knows the truth—*I am afraid*. Not of the storm, but of the silence that has settled between us, and the words I have not spoken.

Five years have passed since you left this place, and five years since I last saw your face. I have watched the waves crash against the rocks, the moon wane and wax, the seasons turn, and still, I have not heard from you. I have not heard your voice, nor seen your eyes. The only thing that has ever brought me comfort in this lonely tower has been the light, steady and unwavering, guiding those who wander the dark.

But tonight—tonight is different. The storm is coming, and I fear it may take more than the light. I fear it may take me.

I have no regrets, Eleanor. I have no bitterness. Only regret that I was not the father you needed. I was a man lost in the sea, blind to the storm that raged within you. I was too proud to see the pain you carried, too stubborn to reach out when you needed a hand. I let the silence grow, and in doing so, I lost you.

I have spent these years in the company of the sea, and it has

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write to you with trembling hands and a heart heavy with years of silence. It has been five long years since you left this house, and five years since I last called you by name. The sea has not been so cruel to me as the silence between us.

Tonight, the wind howls like a beast unleashed, and the waves grow restless, as if they too feel the weight of the storm. I have stood at the window for hours, watching the sky darken with a fury that mirrors the tempest within my soul. I do not know if I shall see dawn. And if I do, I do not know if I shall see you.

I have not written to you in years, not because I do not wish to, but because I have feared the words would not be enough. You were a child once, Eleanor, and I was your father. I tried to be the man you needed. I tried to be the father you deserved. But I failed. I failed to understand you, to follow you, to reach across the chasm that grew between us.

I know you must think me a cruel man, a man who turned his back on you when you needed him most. But I was not cruel. I was broken. I was lost in the sea of my own guilt, in the sorrow of my own sins. You were not the cause of my sorrow, Eleanor. I was. I let my grief over your mother consume

**My Dearest Eliza,**

I write this with the ink trembling in my hand, for the wind howls like a beast in the night, and the sea roars as if it would swallow the very stones of this cliff. The storm is upon us, and I fear it may take more than just the lighthouse.

Five years have passed since you left, and I have not heard a word from you. I have kept the light burning, as I always have, though I no longer feel its purpose. You were the reason I ever kept it lit, you know. The promise I made to your mother, the vow I gave to God and to you. But now, the light seems but a flicker in the dark, and I wonder if I have been fooling myself all along.

I have not written to you, not once. I have not called you by your name, not even in my prayers. I have let the silence grow, like the sea, until it has consumed all that once was between us. I suppose you have no reason to believe this letter is real, and perhaps it is not. Perhaps it is just another ghost of a man who once loved you more than life itself.

But tonight is different. Tonight, the storm is not just a threat to the lighthouse—it is a threat to my very soul. I have seen the sea rise higher than it ever has, and the waves crash with a fury that shakes the very foundations of this

**To My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write to you now with trembling hands and a heart heavy with regret. The wind howls like a wounded beast, and the sea roars as though it seeks to swallow the very earth. The storm is coming, and I fear I may not see the dawn. Yet, in this moment, I must write, for I have never told you the truth of my sorrow.

Five years have passed since you left this house, and five years have not diminished the ache of your absence. I have watched the lantern flicker in the night, and I have heard the waves crash against the rocks, and I have wondered if you ever looked upon the sea and saw it as I do—full of both beauty and sorrow. You were my light, Eleanor, and now I am left in darkness.

I know not what led you to abandon us. I know not if you still remember the days when we sat by the fire and you would read to me, or if you recall the time when you first climbed the stairs to the lantern room and stood beside me, watching the world from above. But I do know this: I have never stopped loving you, and I have never stopped longing for the day when we may speak again.

The storm is upon us now. The winds have risen to a fury, and the sea has turned against us. I have lit the lantern, as I have done for thirty years, and I pray it will guide the ships

**My Dearest Eliza,**

I write this with trembling hands, by the flickering light of the oil lamp, as the wind howls like a wounded beast against the stone walls of this lonely tower. The sea, my dear, is restless tonight—its fury rising with the moon, and I fear the storm may take more than the lighthouse. I pray you are safe, wherever you may be, and that the world has not yet swallowed you whole.

Five years have passed since you left this house, and five years have not softened the ache in my heart. I have tried, Eliza, I have tried to forgive you, to forget the last words we spoke, the way your eyes hardened when I dared to ask you why you had to go. You said you needed to find yourself, to find a life beyond this place, beyond me. But I have never stopped searching for you, and I fear the sea may have taken you before I ever found you again.

I have kept the lighthouse as you once asked, and I have tended the light with the same care you once taught me. The light has guided many ships home, and I have seen the dawn break through even the darkest nights. But tonight, it feels as though the very stars themselves have turned their backs on us.

I have no one but the sea and the wind and the ghosts of the past. I have no one but you, my child. I have written to you, I have

**The Lighthouse, Cliffs of Dunmore**  
**April 12th, 1897**  

My Dearest Eliza,  

I write this with a trembling hand, the wind howling like a wounded beast outside these stone walls. The sea roars in fury, and the sky is a canvas of black and silver, as though the very heavens are tearing themselves apart. I have lit the beacon, as I have done for thirty years, and I pray it will guide those who sail these treacherous waters. But I fear, with every passing moment, that the light may not be enough to save me.  

Five years have passed since you last came to see me, and in that time, the world has changed, and so has I. I have grown old, my back bent by years of toil and sorrow. The lighthouse has been my only companion, and the sea my only confidant. I have watched the tides rise and fall, the stars blink out one by one, and the dawn creep in like a thief. And yet, I have never once thought of you.  

I do not know why I write this now. Perhaps it is the storm, or perhaps it is the weight of silence that has pressed so heavily upon me. I have long since stopped hoping you would return, and I have long since stopped hoping for your forgiveness. But I cannot help but wonder—what have you become in the years since

**To My Dearest Eliza,**

**April 12th, 1889**

**Dear Eliza,**

I write this with trembling hands and a heart heavy with the weight of years unspoken. The wind howls like a wounded beast against the stone walls of this lighthouse, and the sea roars with a fury that mirrors the storm of my soul. I do not know if I shall see the dawn, but if I do, I pray I may find the strength to speak to you once more.

Five years have passed since you left, and five years have felt like a lifetime. I have kept the light burning for you, every night, every storm, every hour of the day. You were my light in the darkness, my compass in the chaos. And now, I fear that light has been extinguished in your heart.

I know not what drove you to walk away from your father, from the only home you ever knew. I have tried to forgive you, and I have tried to forget. But I cannot forget the way your eyes turned from mine, nor the silence that followed your last words. "You don't understand," you said. And perhaps you were right. Perhaps I did not understand. Perhaps I was too blind to see the storm brewing in your soul.

But I do understand now, in a way I never could before. I see the pain that must have been yours, the choices you made, the sacrifices you bore

**My Dearest Eliza,**

I write to you now with trembling hands and a heart full of sorrow, for I know not if I shall live to see the dawn. The sea howls like a beast in pain, and the wind, that old enemy of mine, roars through the cliffs as though it would tear this very lighthouse from its foundations. I have spent my life in its service, and tonight, it seems, the sea wishes to claim me as its own.

Five years have passed since you left this place, and five years have not brought a single word from you. I have not spoken your name aloud in that time, not even to the waves. I have not spoken to anyone. The lighthouse is my only companion now, and the sea, my only friend.

I know you think me a monster, Eliza. You think I have abandoned you, that I have turned my back on the child you once called your mother. But I have not forgotten you. I have not forgotten the day you were born, the way your tiny hand gripped mine as we stood on the shore, the way your eyes, so like your father’s, looked up at me with such trust. I have not forgotten the day you left, nor the silence that followed. I have not forgotten the pain of your silence.

You were young, and I was not the father you needed. I was a man lost in the sea, a man who could not give you

**My Dearest Eliza,**

I write this with trembling hands, the wind howling like a wounded beast against the stone walls of this old lighthouse. The sea roars with a fury I’ve never known, and I fear the night may claim me before dawn. If I do not survive, I pray this letter finds you, and that you read it with a heart not yet hardened by the years we have lost.

Five years, Eliza. Five long, bitter years since you left this house, that sweet, dimly lit room where you once played with your dolls and sang to the sea. I remember the last time we spoke, the way your voice cracked with anger, your eyes like shards of ice. You said I had no right to keep you from your dreams. You said I was a fool, a relic of a world that no longer had a place for me.

And you were right, in a way. I was a fool. I clung to this lamp, this lonely duty, as though it were a tether to a life I had long since abandoned. I let the sea take you, and I let the silence of this place take me too.

But now, as the storm approaches, I find I cannot let go. Not without saying what I should have said long ago. You were my light, Eliza. My only light. I have spent these years in darkness, not because I wished to, but because I thought I had to.

**April 12th, 1889**  
**St. Agnes Lighthouse, Isles of Scilly**  

My Dearest Eleanor,  

If you are reading this, then I have not survived the night. And if I have, then you will know that I have not written this with the intention of being found. But I must, for the sake of the soul I once called mine.  

Five years have passed since the last time we spoke, and yet I find myself thinking of you more than ever. The sea has always been a companion to me, a constant presence, but it is you who has been the storm in my heart—unrelenting, unyielding, and ever-present.  

I do not know what drove you away, nor do I wish to know. You left with your mother, your father's legacy, and your own misguided notions of what it means to be free. I tried to reach you, Eleanor. I sent letters, I wrote and rewrote them, but they were never answered. You did not return for your mother's funeral, nor for the christening of your brother, nor for the day the lighthouse was finally completed. You were never there.  

But tonight—tonight is different. Tonight, the sea is restless. The wind howls like a banshee, and the waves have begun to crash against the cliffs with a fury I have never known. The lighthouse is old, and I

**April 12, 1887**

**To My Dearest Clara,**

I write this with a trembling hand, the wind howling like the souls of the drowned, and the sea roaring as though it would swallow the very rocks upon which I stand. The storm is upon us, and I fear the night may not see me through. Yet, I must write—because if I do not, I fear I will be left with more regrets than I already carry.

Five long years have passed since you left this house, and I have not heard from you since that cold, gray morning when you stood at the door, your eyes filled with fire and your heart with something I could not name. You called me a fool, a man who clung to the past like a sailor to a rope in a gale. I did not answer. I could not. And I have not spoken to you since.

But now, as the heavens weep and the waves crash against the cliffs with a fury I have never known, I find myself thinking of you. Not with anger, not with bitterness, but with a sorrow that has grown heavier with each passing year. You were my light, my only light, and I let it go. I let it go because I was afraid. Afraid that I would not be enough for you. Afraid that I would not understand the world you had come to live in. And so, I shut you out, and in

**To My Dearest Eliza,**

I write this with trembling hands, the wind howling like a wounded beast against the glass of this old lighthouse. The sea, which has always been my constant companion, roars with a fury I have never known. The storm is upon us, and I fear it may take more than the light from this tower.

Five long years have passed since the last time we spoke. Time, as cruel as it is, has not softened the wounds left by our parting. I have not forgotten the day you left, nor the bitterness that followed. You called me a man of stone, unmoved by the pain you bore. I was, and still am, a man of stone—unmoved by the storms of the world, but not by the storms of the heart.

I have kept the light burning every night, as I have always done, for the sake of those who sail these waters. It is my duty, my solemn vow. But tonight, I wonder if the light will be the last thing I see. The sea is rising, and the wind has taken on a voice like a thousand ghosts. I have heard it whisper your name, and I have thought of you more than I have in years.

You were my only child, Eliza. I gave you my name, my heart, and the only home I ever knew. And yet, you chose to leave, as though the world had no place for you, and

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

It has been five long years since I last laid eyes upon you, and I fear time has not been kind to either of us. The sea, as always, has been a cruel companion, and I find myself once more at the mercy of its wrath. Tonight, the wind howls like a wounded beast, and the waves, black and furious, crash against the cliffs with a fury I have not seen in many a year. I am not certain I shall see dawn.

You may think me a fool, or perhaps a man who has long since given up on the world. But I write this not in despair, but in the hope that, should I not survive the night, these words may reach you and offer you some small comfort, some final piece of truth.

You were once my greatest pride, Eleanor. I raised you with care, with love, and with the hope that you would one day understand the weight of duty, the solemnity of responsibility. I was a man of the sea, and the sea is not a place for children. I told you that when you were young, and I saw the way your eyes would follow the lighthouse beam across the dark waters, I knew you had a fire within you, a fire that would not be easily tamed.

But you left. You left without a word, without a farewell. I do not know what drove you, nor do I care to know. You were my daughter,

**April 3rd, 1892**  
**The Lighthouse, St. Agnes Head**  
**Isle of Wight, England**

My Dearest Eleanor,

I write to you now with trembling hands and a heart that aches with the weight of years. Five years have passed since you left this house, and in that time, I have not spoken your name aloud, not even in the quiet hours when the sea howls like a wounded beast. But tonight, the wind is rising, and the sea is restless—more so than I have ever seen it. I fear that the storm may not be the only thing that will take me.

I have not written to you, nor have I sent word of my whereabouts, for I have not known how to begin. The silence between us has been a wall, thick and unyielding, and I have not had the courage to break it. But now, as the sky darkens and the waves grow louder, I feel the need to speak, to be heard, even if it is only in the pages of this letter.

You were a child when you left, Eleanor. A child with a spirit as fierce as the tempests that rage across these shores. I remember the day you stood at the door, your eyes filled with a fire that could not be extinguished. You told me you were going to find your own way, to become something more than a daughter of the lighthouse.

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with the chill of the sea upon my bones and the weight of the storm pressing in from the horizon. The wind howls like a beast in torment, and the waves crash against the rocks with a fury that makes my hands tremble. I fear that tonight may be the last I see of this world, and if it is, I would have you know that I have not forgotten you—nor have I ever stopped loving you.

Five years have passed since the last letter you sent me, and I have waited in silence, hoping that one day, you would come back. But you did not. You did not write, you did not call, and you did not return. I have tried to forgive you, Eleanor. I have tried to believe that the pain of your absence was not of your making. But the silence between us has grown colder than the winter winds that sweep across these cliffs.

I have been a lighthouse keeper for over thirty years, and I have seen many storms. Some have broken the light, others have shattered the lantern, and many have taken lives. But never have I felt so alone as I do now, with the weight of the world pressing upon me and the knowledge that you have turned your back on me.

I know that you blame me for what happened that night—when the sea swallowed the ship and took your mother from us. I know you think I failed you. But I did not. I

**My Dearest Eliza,**

It is with a heart heavy with regret and a mind clouded by the weight of years that I take up my quill to write to you. Five long years have passed since the last time our paths crossed, and though I have not spoken your name in that time, it lingers in my thoughts like the salt air that clings to my skin after a storm.

You remember the last time we spoke. I was in a rage, and you were in tears. I said things I do not now recall, but I fear they were not kind. I do not know what it is that has kept you from returning, but I have come to believe that I have not been the father you deserved. I have been a man of stone and salt, and not a man of love.

Tonight, the skies have darkened with a fury I have not seen in many a year. The wind howls like a wounded beast, and the sea, that old enemy, roars in defiance. I have lit the lamp, as I have done for thirty years, but I fear I may not be able to keep it burning much longer. The storm is upon us, and I do not know if I will live to see dawn.

I have thought of you often in these long nights, when the lighthouse is quiet and the world is still. I have imagined you as a child, playing on the shore, your laughter echoing through the dunes.

**My Dearest Eliza,**

I write this with trembling hands, the wind howling like a wounded beast against the stone walls of this lighthouse. The sea roars with a fury I have never known, and I fear the night may claim me before dawn. Yet, in this moment of reckoning, I find myself compelled to put pen to paper, for I have not spoken your name in five long years, and I fear I may never again.

You were but a child when you left, a little girl with eyes like the sea at twilight, and a heart that beat with dreams too grand for this small, lonely world. I tried to hold you close, to keep you safe, but you were always reaching for the horizon, for something beyond the reach of this shore. I did not understand then, and I still do not. You called me "Father" with a voice that could have softened steel, and yet I stood in your way.

I know you would not have stayed. You were never meant for this life of quiet duty, of salt and smoke and endless nights. You wanted more than the light, more than the sea. You wanted the world. And I, in my foolish pride, thought I could keep you here, in the grip of my own stubborn love.

But you did not stay.

And so, I have been alone these years, with only the beacon and the wind for company. I have watched the stars, and the moon, and

**To My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with trembling hands and a heart heavy with the weight of years unspoken. The wind howls against the lighthouse like a beast in torment, and the sea roars with a fury that matches the storm in my soul. I do not know if I shall see the dawn, but if I do, I shall not be the same man who once held you close in his arms.

Five long years have passed since you left this place, and five years have not been enough to mend the rift between us. I have tried, Eleanor—truly, I have. I have lit the beacon every night, not just for the sailors who rely upon it, but for the quiet moments when I think of you, and the light reminds me of the warmth you once brought into my life.
